sourceReference: Client-supplied HELP 0-3, 2nd Edition strands and skills workbook; pending authoritative approval and full licensed credit notes.
attribution: Imported deterministically in workbook order. Displayed skill codes are intentionally not unique.
disclaimer: Reference content for client review. Do not enable real-data scoring until this artifact and the complete licensed credit criteria are approved as authoritative.

creditDefinitions.label: Present
creditDefinitions.description: The educator confirms that the skill criteria are met.

creditDefinitions.label: Emerging
creditDefinitions.description: The educator confirms emerging performance toward the skill criteria.

creditDefinitions.label: Not observed
creditDefinitions.description: The skill was not observed despite a meaningful opportunity.

creditDefinitions.label: Not applicable
creditDefinitions.description: The educator determines that the skill is not applicable.

twoMinusRule.decisionReference: Pending final client/scientist confirmation; no stopping or inference rule is applied.

skills.skillName: Quiets when picked up–A+ if >20 mins. to console or recover; A- if rarely cries
Responds to sound–attends to, wide range of sounds; A+ if excessive startle, cries; A- if hearing ok but little response
Enjoys & needs a great deal of physical contact and tactile stimulation–relaxes, smiles, looks; A+ usually cries, arches; A- if unaware
Molds and relaxes body when held; cuddles–molds to adult; A+ usually cries, arches; A- limp, passive; also Strand 5-5
Shows pleasure when touched and handled– A+ strong aversion to movement; A- apathetic or needs vigorous movement
Inspects surroundings–A+ if usually cries, shuts eyes to visual stimulation; A- if no interest
Listens to voice for 30 seconds–A+ if distracted, hyper-reactive; A- if hearing ok but rarely interested
Stays awake for longer periods without crying, following individual sleep/feeding patterns; A+ if always fussy, naps <20 minutes
Shows active interest in person / object 1 minute or more–can focus on a variety; A+ if usually distressed A- if unresponsive
Uses hands and mouth for sensory exploration of objects–variety of textures; A+ if avoids; A- if no reaction; Strand 1-1
Stops unexplained crying–can self-console within 15 minutes, e.g., suck fingers, rock self; A+ must be held, often irritable
Enjoys social play–with movement & touch, e.g., nuzzle tummy, "Pat-a-cake"; A+ "falls apart", A- unresponsive; Strand 5-5
Localizes tactile stimulation by touching the same spot or searching for object; A+ if very upset, A- if rarely reaction
Enjoys frolic play–e.g., being held in air; tolerates movement in all planes; A+ if gags, extreme upset; A- no change in affect
Smells different things–no adverse responses to routine odors
Plays 2-3 minutes with a single toy–sustained attention; A+ if > 9 months & unable to focus, A- if depressed, no interest, or only if strong sensory input associated
Listens to speech without being distracted by other sources–attends to speaker; A+ distracted by typical background
Drools less except when teething–A+ if excessive drool, not related to congestion or teething; also in Strand 6-1
Finger feeds self–touches/eats variety of textured foods; A+ frequently gags, avoids; A- craves strong tastes, textures
Sleeps nights 12-14 hrs–usually sleeps through night; falls asleep within 20 mins if tired; A+ rarely sleeps > 1-2 hrs. at time; Strand 6-2
Cooperates with dressing by extending arm, leg–A+ strong reaction to touch of clothing, certain textures
Enjoys messy activities–e.g., water and food play; A+ if always avoids, upset
Reacts to various sensations–A+ gags to certain tastes and textures; extreme preferences
Uses playdough and paints–freely explores; A- if craves, eats, only squeeze
Enjoys solitary play for a few minutes–A+ always distracted, A- "tuned-out" or rigid, repetitive, e.g., spin wheels
Enjoys rough and tumble play–A+ extreme anxiety, A- if passive or frequently craves strong movement
Handles fragile items carefully–A+ purposely breaks A- unintentional breaks
Enjoys tactile books–freely explores
Plays with water and sand–freely explores; A+ if avoids, upset; A+ "craves"; more interested in texture than play
Identifies familiar objects by touch–e.g., spoon, ball hidden in a bag

Enjoys being read to and looks at books independently–able to focus on the "story" and pictures at least 5 mins; A- only perseverate on details; A+ always needs extensive external support to attend more than a few seconds

Participates in circle games; plays interactive games–plays and interacts with 2-3 peers in e.g., "Tag", "Hide & Seek"; A+ always overly aggressive or overwhelmed and "falls apart"; A- if always "tunes out", may wander aimlessly
Sits quietly when listening to stories, music, television, movies, etc.–at least 15 mins. if interested

Quiets down after active period–e.g., able to sit down after playtime; use an "inside" voice after outdoor play; A+ usually tantrums or "falls apart", disrupts family routine; takes more than 15 minutes to recover from transition
Allows hair to be washed–as part of typical home bathing routine; A+ if "falls apart", always a struggle
Begins play with rattles–holds, looks, mouths, a few seconds
Uses hands and mouth for sensory exploration of objects–shakes, mouths, pats, waves; regardless of object
Bangs object on table–hits any surface repetitively with object to explore the action
Plays 2–3 minutes with a single toy–all of above schemes plus examining, e.g., rotating, transfer, poke, tear
Plays with paper–applies complex sensory motor schemes: crumples, tears, pulls
Slides toy or object on surface–another complex motor scheme of "sliding," e.g., toy car, block
Engages in simple relational play–uses 2 different but related objects, e.g., a brush to doll's head; spoon in cup

Demonstrates drinking from cup–plays with objects according to function, e.g., cups are for drinking, dolls are for hugging, car for pushing; A if always preoccupied in non-functional, repetitive interactions, e.g., wobbling cup, spinning wheel
a. symbolic play with realistic props–e.g., uses small broom to sweep, cloth to wipe
b. symbolic play with similar but not real props–e.g., uses stick as a toothbrush, napkin as a blanket
Dramatizes using a doll–links three actions with doll in sequence; acts as if doll can see or hear
Engages in simple make-believe activities–short, discrete pieces of make-believe drama, e.g., playing doctor, having a birthday party; uses abstract props, e.g., table as an oven
Talks intelligently to self–as if pretend person is there; to expand play; N/A if significant speech delay
Plays house–assigns roles to others; more than 3 props (some abstract or imaginary); thematic with several steps, e.g., blanket on doll and sing while rocking

Uses dolls or action figures to act out sequences–seen in real life; multiple steps to the scenario; doll is friend with own personality; e.g., prepare pretend food for dinner with doll, wash up, read a book before bed
Imitates familiar gesture–gesture he can see and is in repertoire, e.g., patting, banging
Imitates new gesture–not part of his repertoire–reproductions frequently not accurate, e.g., rubbing
Imitates several new gestures–at least three; immediate and accurate
Imitates "invisible" gesture–unfamiliar movement not visible to self, e.g., wrinkling nose; pulling earlobe
Imitates several "invisible" gestures–at least 3 invisible unfamiliar gestures, one accurately and directly
Repeats sequence by physical movements–e.g., pat knees, then tap feet; make a fist, clap, then wiggle fingers; sequence of two to three, in imitation
Responds to sounds–reflexive responses, e.g., startles, change in respiration, expression; eyes widening
Responds to voice–e.g., expression brightens or becomes intent; looks at speaker; smiles; slowly turns toward voice; quiets; stops or increases sucking
Searches with eyes for sound–alerting/attending responses; definite eye searching, smiling, moving head to sound source; does not have to find source
Turns eyes and head to sound of hidden voice–finds speaker at side out of immediate sight
Localizes sound with eyes–looks directly; lateral plane to side
a. below after turning head to side–sound at child's waist level; child turns head to side and then looks down
b. below by looking directly–make sound at child's waist; child looks directly to sound
c. above after turning head to side–make sound above shoulder; turns head to side then up
d. above by looking directly–make sound above shoulder; child looks directly up
e. directly above and behind–make sound directly above head, then directly in back
Identifies sounds, words just spoken–"What or who was that?" e.g., animal sounds or car outdoors
Reacts to disappearance of slowly moving object–continues to look at barrier a few seconds after disappears
Finds a partially hidden object–cover hides at least 50 percent of the object
a. one screen–only one cover is present
b. two screens–two covers laying next to each other
c. three screens–three covers laying next to each other
Finds hidden object under three superimposed screens–watches object hidden under three layered covers, pulls off one at a time

Hidden displacement one screen–e.g., with child watching, put Cheerio in your hand, make fist & cover with cloth; when child NOT watching, empty on tray & cover with cloth; show your empty hand; child looks under cloth
Hidden displacement two screens–same as #1.62, but 2nd screen available; goes directly to correct cover
Hidden displacement three screens–same as #1.62, but now under any of 3 covers
Hidden displacement two screens alternately–same as #1.78, alternate screens
Series of hidden displacements; object under last screen–child systematically looks for and finds under the last of three covers after watching adult pass hand under each
Series of hidden displacements; object under 1st screen–same as #1.94 but object left under 1st cover–child searches under third cover first, then middle cover before finding under the 1st
Sequences a 3-picture story that has been read–pictures will clearly depict sequence of the story
Reaches for second object purposefully–when holding one, reaches for 2nd with either hand; may not attain
Works for desired, out of reach object–e.g., may pivot, roll, stretch, wiggle, crawl; does not have to obtain
Retains two of three objects offered–immediately drops one without thought; may not obtain 3rd
Retains two and reaches for 3rd object–intentional thought; e.g., may reach with mouth, lay one block down
Retrieves object using other material–pulls cloth to get out-of-reach object laying at end
Overcomes obstacle to obtain object–moves around obstacle, pushes out of way, or reaches over it
Uses locomotion to regain object; resumes play–e.g., crawls to get block to put in box
Pulls string horizontally to obtain toy–not just to play with string (Safety Alert: only use safe "string-like" attachments, e.g., toy links)
Pulls string vertically to get toy–pulls string up to get dangling toy (Safety Alert: only use safe "string-like" attachments, e.g., toy links)
Inverts small container to obtain tiny object after demonstration–may first poke finger in or shake bottle
Inverts small container spontaneously to obtain tiny object–same as #4.64, no demo; also in Strand 1-5
Solves simple problems using tools–e.g., use stick to get ball under sofa; stool to reach toy on a counter
Opens doors by turning knob–purposefully turns to open, not just playing; also in Strand 6-7
Holds paper with one hand while drawing with the other hand–figures out how to keep paper in place to be able to draw
Answers questions logically–when asked a "what-if" question, e.g., What do you do if you are hungry?
Inspects own hands–to explore movements if < 5 mo; A if > 5 mo. and is preoccupied; also Strands 4-3, 5-2
skills.strand: 1-4 Problem Solving - C. Cause and Effect

skills.skillName: Enjoys repeating newly learned activity–systematically repeat body movement to prolong spectacle, e.g. kick leg to make mobile move; also in Strand 2-4A
skills.strand: 1-4 Problem Solving - C. Cause and Effect

skills.skillName: Continues a familiar activity by initiating movements involved–e.g., wiggles to repeat Pat-a-cake
skills.strand: 1-4 Problem Solving - C. Cause and Effect

skills.skillName: Shows interest in sounds of objects–e.g., uses two different actions with same toy to make different sound
skills.strand: 1-4 Problem Solving - C. Cause and Effect

skills.skillName: Touches toy or adult's hand to restart an activity–but does not need to try to start it as below
skills.strand: 1-4 Problem Solving - C. Cause and Effect

skills.skillName: Guides action on toy manually–rather than using attached switch, key, button
skills.strand: 1-4 Problem Solving - C. Cause and Effect

skills.skillName: Hands toy back to adult–when the action of toy stops; gives to adult to ask for help to restart
skills.strand: 1-4 Problem Solving - C. Cause and Effect

skills.skillName: Attempts and then succeeds in activating mechanical toy–credit at 18-20 mo. WITH demo; 20-22 if no demo
skills.strand: 1-4 Problem Solving - C. Cause and Effect

skills.skillName: Looks from one object to another–1 ft from child's chest, 6-8 inches apart; switches glance several times
Reaches and grasps object–reaches for nearby objects more often than distant objects; also in Strand 4-3

Anticipates visually the trajectory of a slowly moving object–if, when watching an object move slowly in one direction, it moves briefly behind a barrier, child looks to the other side of barrier for it to reappear
Follows trajectory of fast moving object–if object falls to the floor, looks to the floor, does not have to find it
Takes objects out of container–by dumping or taking out one by one
Rotates objects to find functional side–bottle, mirror: rotates and examines three dimensional aspects
Throws objects–to watch the effects when they land
Takes ring stack apart–removes at least one ring by pulling it off upright, or turning base on side to slide off
Puts three or more objects into container–e.g., block in a bowl; also in Strand 4-4

Looks at place where ball rolls out of sight–e.g., if a ball rolls under a skirted sofa, he goes to the place it disappeared to look for it; if not mobile, credit sustained eye pointing or gesturing if observed at least twice
Builds tower using two cubes–places any object on top of another; also in Strand 4-6B
Pulls string vertically to obtain toy–pulls string upwards to get dangling toy; also in Strand 1-4B (Safety Alert: only use safe "string-like" strings, e.g., toy links)
Stacks rings–places at least two rings on ring stack toy; may hold the base to stabilize or tilt if needed
Makes detours to retrieve objects–moves around an obstacle to get an object rather than following the more difficult path, e.g., under a table
Inverts small container spontaneously to obtain tiny object–no demo; also in Strand 1-4B
Brings objects from another room on request–going to or naming the expected location of the requested object; may have a reminder; N/A if motor disability interferes
Rights familiar picture–spontaneously rotates familiar picture upright when presented upside down
Finds detail in favorite picture book–e.g., a bird in the tree, buttons on a suit; at least two
Completes three-four piece puzzle–of a familiar object, animal or person that has three-four related pieces
Points to or places an object on top/bottom–when asked, e.g., touch top of the box, touch the bottom
Names an item when shown parts–object familiar to child, e.g., cut-out picture part of a boat, dog, truck
Looks at named pictures one minute when named–looks intently at one or two; not necessarily a full minute
Enjoys looking at pictures in books–looks with interest at pictures; adult encourages
Pats picture–pats or touches pictures while looking at them
Matches objects to picture–identifies which picture from three or four is the picture of a similar object before him
Points to five-seven pictures of familiar objects/people–recognizes outline pictures e.g., dog, spoon, doll, leaf, bottle, car; A if usually preoccupied on one detail without regard for whole picture
Recognizes familiar adult in photo–points/names three familiar people
Tells story looking at picture book that has been read many times–story has much word repetition
Understands concept of one–can identify a set of "one" from a set of more than one, e.g., "Which hand has just one block?"
Gives one out of many–gives/takes one from a group of similar objects, e.g., "Give me one block" from pile
Understands concept of two–gives or takes "two" of something from a group, e.g., "Give me two blocks" from pile
Identifies and counts quantities of one, two, three or four–e.g., which picture has two ducks? Which picture has three ducks? How many ducks in this picture? Give me three blocks
Shows understanding of color–e.g., looks at the colors of crayons before selecting one
Matches objects–finds toy from group of three that matches model
Sorts objects–sorts mixed group of objects into three piles
Matches shapes–circle, triangle, square; when shown a shape, identifies another shape that matches the model, two shapes are available to choose
Matches colors–black, white; when shown a black or white object, identifies another object of the same color; two objects to choose
Matches identical simple pictures of objects–matches a picture with another exact one; two choices
Matches primary colors–when shown a blue, red, or yellow object, identifies another object of the same color; three to choose from
Matches similar pictures of objects–e.g., picture of Poodle to picture of Collie when paired with a bird
Sorts shapes–circle, triangle, square [toys]; sorts a pile of shapes into three piles according to shape
Sorts colors and points to several colors when named–separates a pile of three different colored similar objects into three groups according to color
Matches like items based on function–e.g., things we clean with, ride in, keep us warm
Shows understanding of size–e.g., puts a small block on top of a large block
Nests two then three cans–e.g., fitting cup or can into a larger one; credit at 12-15 months if two cans; 16-19 months if three cans
Assembles four nesting blocks–four boxes of graduated sizes in correct order; trial-error practice OK
Uses size words–at least one, e.g., "big," "little"; also in Strand 2-3
Stacks rings in correct order–graduated sized rings on a stick, or, graduated sized blocks on top of each other in order
Points to larger or smaller of two spoons–e.g., during snack time ask child to take big cookie, small spoon, little cup; comparative objects are present
Identifies longer stick–one object is at least two inches longer
Begins to pick longer of two lines–e.g., draw a 4 and a 6 inch line, ask the child to touch, draw on or push a miniature car on the "longer" line
Locates big and little objects in groups of 2–e.g., "Which is the big truck? Show me the little ball. Where is the big horse? Point to the little dog."
Matches sounds to animals–at least two; spontaneously or on request
Matches sounds to pictures of animals–two-three; spontaneously; pointing or, on request, e.g., "What does the (pictured animal) say?"
Demonstrates use of objects–e.g., with shoe, telephone, comb, car, child demonstrates or tells if asked, "What do you do with a (name of object)"
Identifies clothing items for different occasions–e.g., boots and raincoats are worn on a rainy day
Identifies objects with their use–e.g., shoe, cup, crayon, spoon, scissors, book, car; child points to "What do we drink out of?" "Which do we wear on our feet?"
Identifies body parts with their function–e.g., "What do we see with?" "hear with?" "smell with?"; child points to or names at least one body part
Begins to respond to opposite analogies–says opposite when a word is presented in leading phrase, e.g., "The fire is hot, ice is...?"; at least two of three opposite analogies
Locates pictured analogy–selects object that does not belong, e.g., picks dog when shown picture card with apple, cookie, bread, dog
Watches speaker's eyes and mouth
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Looks and vocalizes to own name–may not vocalize but readily turns or looks to speaker; A if hears and knows name but never or rarely responds; also in Strand 5-2
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Looks for family members or pets when named–at least three
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Listens selectively to familiar words–familiar words within short phrases, e.g., looks at bottle in response to "Where's your bottle?"; at least three-five words; no gesture cues
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Knows what "no no" means and reacts–e.g., briefly inhibits, cries, puckers lip; also in Strand 5-4
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Shows understanding of words by appropriate behavior or gesture–at least six; e.g., points, looks, gives
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Understands most noun objects–at least 10-15 objects from a variety of categories
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Recognizes and points to four animal pictures–at least four out of six familiar animal pictures/toys
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Points to several clothing items on request–at least four clothing items without gesture cues
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Understands personal pronouns, some action verbs and adjectives–two verbs, two adjectives, and the pronouns: you, your, me, my, and I; does not need gesture cues
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Identifies rooms in own house–goes to, or points correctly to at least two rooms
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Understands complex/compound sentences–involving more than one thought, e.g., "You can have your dessert after you finish eating your beans"
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Selects pictures involving action words–e.g., "sleeping," "eating," "crying," "brushing," at least three
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Listens to stories–e.g., repeats words/phrases from story, gestures indicate interest and understanding
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Understands many action verbs–at least 10; points, names, or demonstrates
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Understands more adjectives–e.g., "hot," "dirty," "happy," "wet," "dry," one, many; at least 8
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Formulates negative reasoning–understands "no" or "not" as meaning non-existence or disappearance (e.g., "No more"), and denial (e.g., "That's not yours," or "That's not a spoon")
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Understands all common verbs, most common adjectives, some prepositions–also various verb tenses, e.g., eat, eating, ate; subtle adjective differences, e.g., warm versus hot; some abstract terms, e.g., scary
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Points to or places object in front, in back, around, through based on verbal cues–e.g., put the ball in front of the box, then in back of the box; push the car around the box; push car through the tunnel
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Points to common objects according to function - verbal cues–e.g., "What do we eat?" "…wear?"
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - A. Objects, Events, and Relationships

skills.skillName: Identifies one body part–major part, e.g., eyes, nose, tummy, foot; on self, doll, or other person
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - B. Body Parts

skills.skillName: Identifies three body parts–major part, e.g., eyes, nose, tummy, foot; on self, doll, or other person
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - B. Body Parts

skills.skillName: Identifies six body parts–may begin smaller parts such as tongue, knee; on self and doll or person
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - B. Body Parts

skills.skillName: Knows more body parts–includes small parts, e.g., tongue, lips, eyebrow; on self and doll or person
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - B. Body Parts

skills.skillName: Points to six body parts on picture of a doll–same as 1.131 above, but on full length picture
skills.strand: 2-1 Understanding the Meaning of Words - B. Body Parts

skills.skillName: Responds to simple requests with gestures–e.g., "Come here," while holding out arms
Responds to simple verbal requests–no gesture cues but in context, e.g., "Drink your juice," when holding cup
Indicates two objects from group of familiar objects–follows a direction involving two objects, e.g., "Give the baby a bottle," if a brush, cup, and block also present
Obeys two part (related) commands–e.g., "Take your napkin and wipe your face."
Obeys two part (separate) commands–e.g., "Give me your spoon and give mommy your cup"
Carries out 3 simple related successive commands in order–e.g., "Wipe your face, give me your napkin, and then get your ball to play ball"
Says "dada" or "mama," specifically–or other consistent label to mean a primary caregiver
Uses expressive vocabulary one-three words–spontaneously, may be approximation; plus "mama" or "dada"
Uses exclamatory expressions–any contextually appropriate exclamation
Says "no" meaningfully–to protest or refuse
Names one or two familiar objects–in response to "What's this?", if in expressive vocabulary
Uses 10-15 words spontaneously–in addition to "mama" or "dada" and exclamatory words; not in imitation; may be approximations
Uses expressive vocabulary of 15-20 words–objects and people, and some action or verb-like words, e.g., "go," "up," and relational descriptors or modifiers such as "hot," "all-gone," "more," "this"
Uses own name to refer to self–formal or "nick-name"; may be approximation
Names two pictures–of familiar objects; on request or spontaneously
Uses nouns, verbs, modifiers–at least 20; includes some verbs and modifiers, e.g., go, open, up, more, gone, dirty, this
Names three pictures–same as #2.56 above, but three
Imitates spontaneously or requests new words–do not credit if child repeatedly says "what's this" without appearing to process, listen to or respond to the answer
Names five pictures–spontaneously or on request
Uses "self-centered" pronouns–e.g., "I," "me," "mine"; also in Strand 5-2
Uses expressive vocabulary of 50+ words–not in imitation; by report and observation; either singly or in multi-word phrases
Uses size words–understands and can say one or two, e.g., "big," "little," "tiny"; also in Strand 1-7B
Refers to self using pronoun–says "I," or "me," may not be grammatically correct; can switch perspectives, e.g., if asked "Do you want juice?" child responds "I want juice"
Verbalizes one preposition–to indicate location, e.g., "in," "out," "on," "off," "under," "next to"
Names eight or more pictures–same as #2.56; may also use simple drawings, pictures in a book
Gives full name on request–first, last name (sometimes middle) when asked "What's your name?"
Uses expressive vocabulary of 200+ words–variety of words, spontaneous, meaningful
Verbalizes two prepositions–e.g., "in," "out," "on," "off," "under," "next to"
Uses expressive vocabulary of 300-1,000 words–do not need to count all
Verbalizes three prepositions–e.g., "in," "out," "on," "off," "under," "next to"
Uses noun plurals; uses verb plurals–e.g., shoes, runs
Names common objects and actions–spontaneously or on request, at least 10 different familiar objects and 10 different familiar actions, e.g., clock, leaf, spoon; washing, sleeping, running
Shows active interest in person/object for at least 1 minute–"communicative readiness" by using gaze and body position to initiate, maintain, terminate or avoid interaction with a person/object
Continues a familiar activity by initiating movements involved–e.g., when the parent stops rocking or bouncing the child, the child wiggles to continue; also in Strand 1-4C
Lifts arms to parent–to signal desire to be picked up or "rescued" from an unfamiliar situation; also in Strand 5-1
Waves or responds to bye-bye–may be an approximation of a true wave
Extends toy to show others, not for release–to say "look at," acknowledge, share, or explain it
Understands pointing–and uses to say "What's that?", "Look!", "I want"; alternates gaze to person and object
Greets with verbal cues–waves to say "bye" or "hi"; does not need to see model
Gives toy to familiar adult spontaneously and upon request–for the purpose of initiating an interaction, not because he is no longer interested; also in Strand 5-5
Gestures spontaneously to indicate needs–pointing, showing, extending arms or hand, taking adult hand; also combines gestures; credit in Strand 2-4B if also vocalizes
Points to distant object outdoors–to get attention, request info., or to exclaim
Interacts with peers using gestures–sometimes aggressive, e.g., pushing, pulling or grabbing; also Strand 5-5
Cry varies in pitch, length and volume to indicate needs, e.g., hunger, pain–easier to interpret
Responds to sound stimulation or speech by vocalizing–makes any sound to "take a turn"
Vocalizes attitudes other than crying–joy, anger; e.g., squeal, grunt, whimper, coo; same as 5.13, Strand 5-3
Babbles to people–to start or respond to a "conversation" or attract attention
Shouts for attention–no credit if by whining, screaming, crying; N/A if not present but uses, e.g., babble, smiles
Babbles in response to human voice–to communicate a variety of intentions; takes at least three "turns"
Experiments with communication–not frustrated when not understood; N/A if not present
Vocalizes spontaneously to indicate needs–may also credit in Strand 2-4A if gestures are used
Uses voice in conjunction with pointing or gesturing–to make a comment, request, answer or acknowledge
Vocalizes wishes and needs at the table; names desired items–e.g., "more," "all gone," "down," and "no," plus one or two food, drink or utensil items visible but out of reach
Echoes prominent or last word spoken–sometimes to help remember and process; A if extreme, perseverative; N/A if not present
Tells experience using jargon and words–e.g., if toy "breaks" during play (e.g., wheel off a car, broken crayon); ask child to tell parent what happened

Experiments with communication–frustrated when not understood; may cry, whine, shout or give up; N/A if not present; A if extreme–always "falls apart" or only unusual sounds without communicative intent
Relates experiences using short sentences–two-three word sentence to tell about a past experience; can stay on topic for two or more turns; sentence structure or grammar may be incorrect
Answers questions–"What," "Where," "Who" type questions, but usually not "Why"; more than yes/no response
Frustrated if not understood–utterances have communicative intent; mark A if utterances do NOT have communicative intent or frustration is extreme; N/A if not frustrated
Vocalizes for all needs–effectively; does not need to rely on gestures or whining
Participates in storytelling–helps tell a favorite story: adding words, making comments, anticipating events, etc.
Relates experiences more frequently using short sentences–same as #2.73 but now regularly engages in conversations about the past, present and near future
Asks questions beginning with "what," "where," "when"–to seek information, initiate or maintain a conversation, e.g., "What's that?", "When see daddy?," "Where is baby?"
Carries on a conversation–as give & take interaction, tells ideas to others, listens to others' ideas
Uses single-word sentences–e.g., "ma ma" means "Mama pick me up"
Imitates two-word phrases–in imitation of model, e.g., "More juice," "Hi Daddy"; do not credit "Thank you," "Bye-bye," "Good girl"
Uses two-word sentences–NOT in imitation, e.g., "go bye-bye," "push car," "doggie eat"; NOT "bye-bye," "good girl," "all gone"
Imitates four-word phrases–e.g., if parent says "Mommy is brushing her hair," the child may respond "Mommy is brushing hair?"
Uses three-word sentences–any word order to express semantic relationship, e.g., object-action; object-location; possession
Uses past tense–may use –ed incorrectly, e.g., "runned," "goed"
Uses plurals–may use incorrectly, e.g., "foots" or "feets"
Over regulates and systematizes plurals and verbs [foots, doed]–e.g., "I doed it!," "I eated the cookie"
Repeats five-word sentences–4 or 5 words, sometimes; in imitation or spontaneous; e.g., asked "did you go to birthday party?", child responds "go to birthday party"
Uses pivot verb "is" to form kernel sentences–e.g., "this is a cat", "the toy is broken"
Uses past tense by adding "ed" to verb–e.g., pushed, jumped, skipped
Uses four to six words in a sentence–spontaneously, no need for model or hints
Describes action in a picture using the present tense–e.g., "The boy is jumping", "Doggy is running", "The baby is sleeping"
Cry is monotonous, nasal, one breath long–vowel-like, nasal quality; tongue "cupped"
Makes comfort sounds–reflexive vocal, automatic; may sound like clicks, short friction noises, or quiet "mmm," "nnn," "ah" sounds
Makes sucking sounds–usually reflexive, not purposeful
Cries more rhythmically with mouth opening and closing–up and down pitch intonations
Coos open vowels [aah], closed vowels [ee], diphthongs [oy as in boy]–often influenced by body movement
Disassociates vocalizations from bodily movement–soft sounds without trunk, arm, or leg movement
Babbles consonant chains "baba-baba"–three or more duplicated syllables, e.g., "baba-baba"
Babbles double consonants "baba"–two syllable repetitions, e.g., "baba" or "dada"
Says "dada" or "mama," nonspecifically–without meaning
Vocalizes in interjectional manner–short, loud sounds of varying pitch that sound like exclamation of glee or surprise
Babbles with inflection similar to adult–inflections sound like questions and commands
Babbles single consonant "ba"–at least one single syllable consonant-vowel utterance
Produces these sounds frequently in babbling: b, m, p, d, t, n, g, k, w, h, f, v, th, s, z, l, r–at least first seven consonant sounds during vocal play or in words
Babbles monologue when left alone–to practice; not necessarily with any intent
Speech may plateau as child learns to walk–may or may not babble less or not learn new words; N/A if not present
Unable to talk while walking–may be unable to use speech while walking; N/A if able to talk while walking

Omits final and some initial consonants–distorts or omits ending and some beginning consonant sounds, e.g., "ca" for "cat," "ba" for "ball," "poon" for "spoon"; A if >18 months & usually omits, distorts initial consonants
Babbles intricate inflection–beginning of jargon: various inflectional patterns and sound combinations that sound like conversation
Makes sounds in babbling, but often substitutes those sounds in words–e.g., says "sh" sound in babbling but not to say "shoe"; A if >18 months & usually omits, distorts initial consonants
Jabbers tunefully at play–same as #2.34 "Babbles intricate inflection"
Uses jargon with good inflection and rate–same as #2.34 but more true words appear
Imitates environmental sounds–animal, nature, machine or vehicle
Uses intelligible words about 65 percent of the time–more than 50 percent of words understood by family members and strangers; pronunciation not always exact
Uses elaborate jargon–interspersed with words; N/A if not present but has vocabulary of at least 15-20
Produces the following sounds clearly: p, b, m, k, g, w, h, n, t, d–each used in initial position of words, and b, p, m, n at end of words; mark A if only 2 vowels limited, or vowels are distorted
Produces sounds correctly at beginning of words–same as #2.65 plus vowels
Replaces jargon with sentences
Repeats words and sounds–repeats his first word in a sentence or question, or the first sound in a word; not excessive; A if increase in severity over many months, struggles; N/A if not present
Uses intelligible words about 80 percent of the time–can usually be understood, even by strangers with similar dialectical background; pronunciation not always exact

Imitates common syllables–consonant + vowel (short), e.g., "mi" as in mit, "pa" as in pat, "fe" as in fed; and vowel (short) + consonant sounds, e.g., "ib" as in rib, "es" as in dress, "ud" as in mud, "og" as in jog
Reacts to music by cooing–may quiet, become very still, coo, and/or look
Moves to rhythms–e.g., bouncing, rocking, moving arms about, twisting, or moving head side-to-side
Attempts to sing sounds to music–more like talking, quiet vocal play, or yelling
Attempts to sing songs with words–with two-three words, sings the last word of a line, or imitates a repetitive section
Enjoys nursery rhymes, nonsense rhymes, fingerplays, poetry–e.g., "Where is Thumpkin," "Wheels on the Bus"; participates with gestures or singing
Sings phrases of songs–"sings along" imitating two-four word phrases heard repetitively, e.g., "Jingle Bells," "Happy Birthday"
Recites a few nursery rhymes–may need some verbal, gesture or picture prompts; can say at least three lines
Moves body to music–sway, clap, or movement that indicates a "feel" for the music
Claps to beat of familiar songs or to speech patterns–in cadence with the repeating phrases
Participates in singing songs with group–singing does not need to be in tune or exact lyrics
Holds head to one side in prone–able to rest with head turned fully to each side; A if 'stuck' or only one side
Lifts head in prone–1-2 seconds; entire face off the surface; A if head always tilted
Holds head up 45 degrees in prone–holds head up, chin 2-3 inches above surface; few seconds
Extends both legs–mark A if "frog-like" or stiff posture; A if arms held flexed & 'trapped' under chest
Rotates and extends head–turns head to each side at least 45 degrees with no head bobbing
Holds chest up in prone–holds head and chest off surface; weight on forearms; holds upper chest off

Holds head up 90 degrees in prone–holds head up in midline, face at 90 degree angle to surface, few seconds; mark A if supports head in hyperextension (as if looking at ceiling, back of head on upper back)
Bears weight on hands in prone–entire chest is raised from surface with weight supported on palms; A if excessive head bobbing, stiff legs, asymmetry, elbows behind shoulders

Holds weight on one hand in prone–maintains weight on one hand (palm side) and abdomen, with arm extended and chest off the surface to reach with opposite arm; credit A if only one side, or using back of hand
Turns head to both sides in supine–may have preference but should turn head easily
Extends both legs–credit A if in a frog-like or stiff position
Kicks reciprocally–uses both legs equally; A if stiff, moves legs together or one but not the other
Assumes withdrawal position–moves in and out of flexion easily
Brings hands to midline in supine–both arms move symmetrically to chest, face; also in Strand 4-5
Looks with head in midline–arms and legs symmetrical
Brings feet to mouth–both feet easily toward face; legs slightly flexed; A if buttocks not raised off surface
Raises hips pushing with feet in supine–do not encourage or teach; A if uses as means of locomotion; N/A if not observed
Lifts head in supine–lifts head slightly, chin tucked toward chest briefly
Struggles against supine position–not an item to elicit/teach; N/A if not observed
Holds head steady in supported sitting–head upright 1 minute, no bobbing
Sits with slight support–trunk fairly upright (some rounding); support at waist
Moves head actively in supported sit–moves head freely, no bobbing, in line with trunk
Sits momentarily leaning on hands–few seconds; hands on floor or slightly flexed legs
Holds head erect when leaning forward–propped as in #3.29, head upright and steady
Sits independently indefinitely, may use hands–steady and erect; can use both hands to play
Sits without hand support for 10 minutes–may use variety of sitting positions; does not need to prop
Bears some weight on legs–briefly; adult provides most of support
Bears almost all weight on legs–adult is providing less support than in #3.23
Bears large fraction of weight on legs and bounces–actively bounces few times; minimal adult support
Stands, holding on–several seconds at chest high support; hands only for balance; not leaning
Stands momentarily–1 or 2 seconds; legs spread widely, arms at "high guard"
Stands a few seconds–same as #3.60 but more than 3 seconds
Stands alone well–head and trunk erect; arms free to play; A if always on toes, asymmetrical
Rolls side to supine–side to back
Rolls prone to supine–from stomach to back; left and right; A if only with strong arching or to one side
Rolls supine to side–initiates roll with head, shoulder or hip; A if only with strong arching or to one side
Rolls supine to prone–back to tummy; some segmental movement; A if only with strong arching or to one side
Circular pivoting in prone–at least 1/4 turn each direction; using arms and legs; A if legs do not participate
Brings one knee forward beside trunk in prone–hip and knee flex up to one side when weight shifts to the opposite side to reach a toy or attempt to move
Crawls backward–not an item to teach; N/A if not observed
Crawls forward–a few feet on belly by moving both arms and both legs; A if legs do not participate
Goes from sitting to prone–through a brief side-sitting position
Assumes hand-knee position–with chest and belly off surface, several seconds
Gets to sitting without assistance–via sidelying or hands and knees
Makes stepping movements–in place; support is used for balance only
Pulls to standing at furniture–arms do most of the work; legs may straighten together or one at a time through a brief half kneel
Lowers to sitting from furniture–without falling or plopping down quickly
Creeps on hands and knees–belly off ground moves in reciprocal pattern several feet; A if 'bunny hops'
Walks holding onto furniture–moves sideways; without leaning; four steps
Pivots in sitting–twists to pick up objects; 180 degrees by using hands for support and twisting trunk
Creeps on hands and feet–not an item to elicit/teach; N/A if not observed
Walks with both hands held–few steps, trunk upright, both hands held only for balance
Stands by lifting one foot–pulls up to stand at support through half-kneel
Assumes and maintains kneeling–bears full weight on knees, not on feet or floor
Walks with one hand held–four steps forward, holding hand only for balance
Walks alone two to three steps–arms in "high guard" position
Falls by sitting–when tires or loses balance, "plops" to floor into sitting
Stands from supine by turning on all fours–no support; series of transitional movements
Creeps or hitches upstairs–at least two steps; creeps or "hitch up," i.e., sitting on steps and pushing up on bottom
Walks without support–across a room; arms to side; can stop, start, turn; A if asymmetric, knees 'locked'
Stoops and recovers–with control by bending his knees and then returns to stand
Neck righting reactions–head is turned to one side when supine, body automatically rolls in same direction; A if >6 months & strongly present, interferes with segmental roll
Flexor withdrawal inhibited–does not automatically pull leg up if sole of foot scratched
Extensor thrust inhibited–does not strongly extend legs when pressure applied to soles
ATNR inhibited–does not automatically move arms and legs into a fencer position when head turns to one side; A if still present >6 months or obligatory at any age
Body righting on body reaction–initiates roll with hip, back to stomach; A if always 'flips'
Moro reflex inhibited–little movement of arms in response to sudden loss of backwards head control; A if still present >6 months
Protective extension of arms & legs downward–if lowered quickly to floor, extends arms and legs; A if asymmetrical or if >7 months & delayed or absent responses
Demonstrates balance reactions in prone–curves trunk in opposite direction of tilt; A if asymmetrical
Protective extension of arms to side and front–extends arms symmetrically to front or side; A if asymmetrical or not present after 9 mo.
Demonstrates balance reactions in supine–moves body in opposite direction of tilt; A if asymmetrical
Demonstrates balance reactions in sitting–moves body in opposite direction of tilt; A if asymmetrical
Protective extension of arms to back–extends one or both arms behind to protect from fall
Demonstrates balance reactions on hands/knees–curves trunk in the opposite direction of the tilt; A if asymmetrical
Demonstrates balance reactions in kneeling–moves in opposite direction of tilt
Lifts head when held at shoulder–momentarily; no support to head or neck
Holds head in same plane as body when held in ventral suspension–holds head in line with trunk
Holds head beyond plane of body when held in ventral suspension–head above trunk; back straight, hips flexed down
Extends head, back and hips when held in ventral suspension–head held above trunk at least 45 degrees, facing forward, hips extended, back straight
Holds head in line with body – pull to sit–no head lag
Lifts head and assists when pulled to sitting–"helps" by flexing arms & immediately lifting head
Extends head, back, hips and legs in ventral suspension–holds head at 90 degree angle to trunk, back straight, hips extended, legs at same level of back, face forward
Bends over and looks through legs–e.g., to pick up ball that rolls slightly behind him; N/A if not observed
Demonstrates balance reactions in standing–on tilt board or mattress
Walks into large ball while trying to kick it–ball should move forward
Stands on one foot with help–each foot, 2-3 seconds, both hands held
Picks up toy from floor without falling–stoops or squats and then returns to stand; no support
Kicks ball forward–no support, either foot
Squats in play–feet flat on floor, does not use hands for balance or propping, able to resume standing
Stands from supine by rolling to side–rather than turning fully to hands and knees
Stands on tiptoes–voluntary 2-3 seconds; should not be a typical posture
Imitates one foot standing–momentarily without support, each foot
Imitates simple bilateral movements of limbs, head and trunk–e.g., Simon Says arms: up, down, front, back, out to side, crossed in front
Stands from supine using a sit-up–rather than rolling to side as in #3.104 above
Stands on one foot one-five seconds–no support, each foot
Kicks large ball when it has been rolled directly to him–kicks while ball is still moving; 10" ball
Balances on each foot for 5 seconds, without support–with eyes open; arms in any position
Walks sideways–no support, e.g., while pulling toy on a string
Walks backwards–few steps while facing forward
Runs–hurried walk; one foot always on ground; high guard posture
Pulls toy behind while walking–e.g., while pulling toy on string
Carries large toy while walking–with one or two hands, e.g., small trash can, pillow, stuffed animal
Pushes and pulls large toys or boxes–e.g., small chair, stool, large box; on smooth surface
Runs fairly well–arms not in high guard posture
Walks with legs closer together–mature gait; feet aligned under shoulders
Runs–stops without holding and avoids obstacles; e.g., stops before wall, does not use hands
Walks on tip-toes a few steps–should be voluntary; not preferred method
Walks backward ten feet–general direction, facing forward
Walks on tiptoes ten feet–voluntarily; may have some walking steps
Avoids obstacles in path–small and large; walking or running, moves around or steps over
Runs on toes–coordinated fashion; both feet leave ground
Makes sharp turns around corners when running–turns and stops with control
Walks 20-foot diameter circle staying on path–keeps feet within 4" of line, heel-toe, alternating steps
Jumps in place both feet–both feet leave floor simultaneously, any height; one of three tries
Jumps a distance of 8 to 14 inches–standing broad jump; may have lead foot; one of several tries
Jumps from bottom step–6-7 inch step; without assistance; both feet together
Jumps backwards–approx. 1 inch; both feet together
Jumps sideways–each direction, approx. 1 inch; both feet together
Jumps on trampoline with adult holding hands–both feet lifting off surface, two consecutive jumps
Jumps over thin barrier 2 to 8 inches high–e.g., block, toy train; lifts and lands with both feet
Hops on one foot–either foot, 2-3 consecutive hops; forward or in place
Jumps a distance of 14 to 24 inches–longest of several tries, one foot may lead; crouches and swings arms
Jumps a distance of 24 inches to 34 inches–same as #3.134 but at least 24 inches
Step-hops for five feet–after demo; on one foot, without assistance at least three seconds; then other foot
Backs into small chair or slides sideways–independently; within a few seconds
Climbs forward on adult chair, turns around and sits–without assistance
Goes up and down slide–small slide; climbs ladder/slides down, without assistance
Climbs jungle gyms and ladders–e.g., swing by hands on low bar and jump down; uses good motor planning
Walks upstairs with one hand held–3 steps, one hand free; two feet per step
Walks upstairs holding rail–both feet on step; 4 steps up; hand on rail or wall, two feet per step
Walks downstairs holding rail–both feet on step; down 3 steps, holding rail or wall; one hand free
Walks downstairs with one hand held–down 3 steps, one hand free, two feet per step
Walks upstairs alone–both feet on step; 4 steps, no support; two feet per step
Walks downstairs alone–both feet on step; 3 steps; no support; two feet per step
Walks upstairs alternating feet–up 4 steps; one foot per step; alternating forward foot
Walks downstairs alternating feet–down 4 steps; one foot per step; alternating forward foot
Throws underhand in sitting–small ball, definite forward fling; standing or sitting
Throws ball forward–in standing; over or underhand, a few feet
Throws overhand within 3 feet of target–while standing; lands within 3 feet to either side
Throws ball into a box–in standing; box 3 feet away; one of three tries
Catches large ball–in standing; traps in chest
Catches eight inch ball–with arms bent; one of two tries; may not occur until 4 years
Bounces and catches a large ball–10-inch ball at least once; catches as ball bounces up
Moves on "ride on" toys without pedals–mounts and propels forward, without assistance
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - G. Riding a Tricycle

skills.skillName: Rides tricycle–move forward few feet using pedals; feet sometimes pushes floor
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - G. Riding a Tricycle

skills.skillName: Uses pedals on tricycle alternately–pedals 4-6 feet forward; may not steer well
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - G. Riding a Tricycle

skills.skillName: Rides tricycle with pedals to maneuver around obstacles, turn, stop and start
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - G. Riding a Tricycle

skills.skillName: Walks with assistance on 8-inch board–8 inch wide board; walks 3 feet holding adult hand
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Walks independently on 8-inch board–8 inch wide board; walks 6 feet independently
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Tries to stand on 2-inch balance beam–steps up with one foot, no help; feet perpendicular to beam
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Walks a few steps with one foot on 2-inch balance beam–without assistance; one foot on, one off
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Walks on line in general direction–10 feet; one foot on or near line
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Walks between parallel lines 8 inches apart–about 10 feet; feet may be on but not completely out of lines
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Stands on 2-inch beam with both feet–two to three seconds, feet in any direction
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Attempts step on 2-inch balance beam–tries to take one step forward with demonstration
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Alternates steps part way on 2-inch balance beam–at least two alternating steps forward
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Keeps feet on line for 10 feet–at least 3 feet, but may sometimes walk up to 10 feet; one foot in front of other, does not step off; may be after two tries
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Walks 10 feet on 4-inch wide line–after demo, may hold arms and head in any position
skills.strand: 3-7 Advancing Postural Control - H. Balance Beam

skills.skillName: Regards colorful object momentarily–few seconds; 8-10 inches from chest
Follows with eyes moving person while in supine–within a few feet
Regards colorful object for few seconds–4 or 5 seconds, 8-10 inches away from his chest
Stares and gazes–may spend half of waking hours with vague indirect expression; N/A if not present; A if >2 months and avoids or rarely directly looks at people or things
Blinks at sudden visual stimulus–if stimulus is too bright, close, abrupt; A if frequent and no stimulus
Follows with eyes to midline–slowly from side he is facing to midline, then "loses" it; may be somewhat jerky
Follows with eyes past midline–a few inches past midline but then "loses" it
Follows with eyes 180 degrees–at least twice, smooth eye movements
Follows with eyes downward–slowly downward from eye level toward chest
Follows with eyes upward–about 10 inches away; moves slowly upward from his chest to his brow
Follows with eyes moving object in supported sitting–and people/pets from a few feet away
Follows with eyes without head movement–in all directions; no or minimal head or body movement
Regards tiny object–as small as a raisin from about 1-2 feet away; reaches, swipes, or looks intently
Looks at distant objects–few feet away, e.g., picture on wall
Indwelling thumb no longer present–typically not held tightly close to palm; A if >5 months always indwelling
Uses ulnar palmar grasp–small objects against palm using middle, ring and little finger; flexed wrist; not thumb
Uses palmar grasp–holds small object against palm with fingers, not thumb
Uses radial palmar grasp–holds small object against palm with fingers and opposing thumb
Attempts to secure tiny object–e.g., cereal bit; using a few fingers or whole hand; makes contact but is usually unsuccessful; A if only fist
Rakes tiny object–e.g., cereal bit; raking or scooping motion; usually attains
Uses radial digital grasp–to pick up small block; thumb opposes; uses tips of fingers, no palm; A if flexed wrist
Uses inferior pincer grasp–picks up a tiny object between thumb pad and the side of his index finger; rests forearm on the surface
Pokes with index finger–can isolate index finger from other fingers; A if only fist
Uses neat pincer grasp–tips of index finger and thumb in opposition; does not need to rest forearm
Grasps crayon adaptively–thick crayon, fist grasp; may not make marks
Points with index finger–holds remaining fingers and thumb close to hand
Holds crayon with thumb and fingers–rather than with a fisted hand; may revert to a fist grasp if heavy marks
Holds pencil with thumb and fingers–adult-like grasp; thumb, index, middle fingers; may move hand as a unit
Activates arms on sight of toy–arms become active immediately upon seeing a toy; stop when toy removed
Inspects own hands–watches movements of his own hands; also in 1-4C; A* if >5 mo & spends excessive time
Reaches toward toy without grasping–backhand approach, object to side; may contact but not grasp
Reaches for toy followed by momentary grasp–with both arms, hands are now open; object at midline; makes contact; sometimes able to grasp briefly; may initially under-reach
Reaches and grasps object–reaches with an angular approach using both arms, hands open, one hand grasping and the other joining; may initially under-reach
Recovers object–dropped within his visual and reaching range, or where he can feel or hear it
Reaches and grasps object with extended elbow–i.e., with a straight arm; typically unilateral; direct, accurate; A if hand fisted or only reaches with both arms as unit or cannot reach at midline
Extends wrist–i.e., bending hand at wrist slightly upwards; A if strongly splays/overextends fingers
Supinates forearm–palm facing upward
Grasps toy actively–voluntarily for several seconds
Keeps hands open 50 percent of time–appear relaxed when at rest
Grasp reflex inhibited–does not automatically close his hand tightly around objects that touch his palm
Keeps hands open most of the time–during rest and when exploring objects
Drops object–'lets go' incidentally or when an adult removes it; no longer obliged by grasp reflex
Transfers object–+ at 7 mo. if directly from one hand to other; +/- at 5-6 mo. if 2-stage transfer; Strand 4-5
Manipulates toy actively with wrist movements–by twisting or rotating forearm and flexing & straightening wrist
Drops objects systematically–repeatedly drops to practice release; more than incidental as in 4.33 above
Releases object voluntarily–with control on a surface
Puts objects in container–does not need to put hand in or lean it on container
Puts three or more objects into small container–small objects; also in Strand 1-5
Puts many objects into container without removing any–at least 6 pellet sized objects jar, bowl; A if after 15 mo., exaggerated finger splaying after release
Puts tiny object into small container–size of a pellet into a narrow necked bottle
Moves arms symmetrically–both equally in a random smooth manner
Brings hands to midline in supine–also in Strand 3-2; both arms near face or chest
Clasps hands–fingers one hand with the other
Looks with head in midline–to look at an object held above his chest; symmetrical arms, legs; also in Strand 3-2
Reaches for object bilaterally–moving both arms as a unit; A if obligatory after 8 months and small object
Places both hands on bottle–spontaneously or initial prompting; 3 seconds; also in Strand 6-3
Retains small object in each hand–few seconds; may place 2nd object in child's hand
Reaches for object unilaterally–only one arm if object small; observe left and right side
Transfers object–see this skill in Strand 4-4
Bangs two cubes held in hands–both objects together at midline
Unwraps a toy–loosely wrapped object using both hands
Uses both hands freely; may show preference for one–does not neglect, avoid one hand during activities
Uses both hands in midline–one holds, other manipulates; at midline cooperatively
Watches adult scribble–at least a few seconds
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Tries to imitate scribble–some contact with the crayon on paper; marks do not need to be visible
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Marks paper with crayon–a back and forth motion; at least three visible marks
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Scribbles spontaneously–spontaneously with definite marks
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Imitates vertical stroke–upward or downward stroke in a general vertical direction, after demo
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Imitates circular scribble–after watching someone scribble in continuous circles
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Imitates horizontal stroke–can switch the direction of his marks to imitate a horizontal stroke; may be longer or shorter than demonstration, and not exactly horizontal
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Imitates a cross–should be closer to a + than an X, lines may be longer, shorter and uneven
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Makes first designs or spontaneous forms–makes definite and purposeful controlled markings that look like he is trying to make pictures or letters
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Copies a circle–from a model, no demo; uses one circular motion; may be approximation
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Draws recognizable face with eyes, nose, mouth–in approximate facial position, no prompting
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Draws square, imitating adult–sides about equal, all corners meet at right angles
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Colors within the lines of a circle–at least 1/2 of a drawn 4-6 inch circle; no more than 2 marks outside
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - A. Pre-Writing

skills.skillName: Places one block on top of another without balancing–may hold on to 2nd block, or if lets go, may fall
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - B. Block Construction

skills.skillName: Builds tower using two cubes–in imitation or spontaneous; balances, releases 2nd block
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - B. Block Construction

skills.skillName: Builds tower using three cubes–in imitation or spontaneous; balances, releases 3rd block
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - B. Block Construction

skills.skillName: Builds tower using four cubes–in imitation or spontaneous; balances, releases 4th block
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - B. Block Construction

skills.skillName: Builds tower using six cubes–in imitation or spontaneous; balances, releases last block
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - B. Block Construction

skills.skillName: Imitates three block train using cubes–aligns three small blocks horizontally and pushes them like a train in imitation; does not need to be exact
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - B. Block Construction

skills.skillName: Builds tower using eight cubes–in imitation or spontaneous; balances, releases last block
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - B. Block Construction

skills.skillName: Imitates three block bridge using cubes–reproduces an exact replica of a three-block bridge after watching an adult build bridge; leaves a small space between the two bottom blocks
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - B. Block Construction

skills.skillName: Builds tower using nine cubes–note if attention interferes
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - B. Block Construction

skills.skillName: Reproduces a two-dimensional block design–matches block color pattern & the design shape on picture and block model, e.g., tower of 3 blue blocks with a yellow top block, aligned next to tower of 3 green blocks
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - B. Block Construction

skills.skillName: Removes round piece from formboard–out of a three shape formboard
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - C. Formboard

skills.skillName: Places cylinders in matching hole in container–no demo; only two holes exposed at a time
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - C. Formboard

skills.skillName: Places round piece in formboard–no demonstration, verbal or gestural cues; may use trial and error
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - C. Formboard

skills.skillName: Places square piece in formboard–places round and square shapes, no demo, verbal or gestural cues; may use trial and error
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - C. Formboard

skills.skillName: Places triangular piece in formboard–no demo, verbal or gestural cues
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - C. Formboard

skills.skillName: Puts together simple inset puzzles–four to six-pieces, one shape per space, e.g., formboard as in 1.114 with diamond and oval, or familiar pictures of objects (ball, cup, etc.)
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - C. Formboard

skills.skillName: Pastes on one side–applies paste to either side of at least two shapes and pastes it on another paper; may have verbal and gestural prompts
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - D. Paper Activities

skills.skillName: Paints within limits of paper–large sheet of paper (approximately 18x22 inches), a paintbrush or crayon
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - D. Paper Activities

skills.skillName: Folds paper imitatively, not precisely–may not be able to crease the fold and the fold may not be in half
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - D. Paper Activities

skills.skillName: Folds paper in half–does not have to make a definite crease but should be in half
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - D. Paper Activities

skills.skillName: Pastes on appropriate side–at least two of three shapes onto another paper, with or without a demonstration
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - D. Paper Activities

skills.skillName: Folds paper three times–after demo, folds paper in half, in half again, and in half again; edges nearly even
skills.strand: 4-6 Spatial Perception and Planning - D. Paper Activities

skills.skillName: Helps turn pages–flips after the adult has separated and lifted it
Turns two or three pages at a time–by pushing or flipping the pages over while his other hand holds or tries to stabilize the book; may be non-functional
Turns pages one at a time–both hands work cooperatively as one hand stabilizes the book and his empty hand turns the pages; thick or regular pages
Removes pegs from pegboard–1/2 inch diameter, at least three pegs by pulling up
Places one round peg in pegboard–1 inch diameter pegs, no demo
Places six round pegs in pegboard–1/2 inch, places before removing any
Places six square pegs in pegboard–1/2 inch diameter, pegs into the pegboard before removing any; N/A if square pegs not available
Strings one bead–by holding the bead in one hand as he threads a heavy string with the other; may not be able to pull the string out fully
Strings three beads–"threads" and pulls string out
Strings smaller beads–at least one small bead or Cheerio, pulling the string out through the other side after threading it without verbal prompting
Snips with scissors–one snip in one place; holds paper in other hand
Snips on line using scissors–using a forward snipping movement rather than continuous cutting motion

Cuts across paper following a straight line 6 inches long and then a curved line 6 inches long–after demo, drawn black line; cuts within 1/4 inch of line using a snipping movement and holding paper in other hand
Cuts out a circle with scissors–paper with drawn circle at least 6 inch diameter; cuts within 1/2 inch of the line using a forward snipping movement and holding paper in other hand
Enjoys and needs a great deal of physical contact and tactile stimulation–cries, looks, or smiles to establish contact; A if needs constant holding or always actively avoids
Establishes eye contact–usually easily, few seconds at a time; A if always actively averts; also in Strand 5-5
Draws attention to self when in distress–cries or fusses to attract attention and approach; A if extreme
Awakens or quiets to parent's voice–shows differentiated and preferential responses to parents' voice: e.g., 'stills', smiles, quiets, or turns; A if always negative or apathetic responses
Socializes with strangers/anyone–e.g., smile, vocalize, enjoy game, after a few moments of warming up
Discriminates strangers–e.g., may delay or not smile to a stranger but readily smiles to parent

Recognizes parent visually–shows differentiated and preferential responses when he sees parent, e.g., smile more, excite, reach for, watch, vocalize and/or fuss; A if usually apathetic, ignores, or averts gaze
Lifts arms to parent–to be picked up or "rescued" from an unfamiliar or strange situation; also in Strand 2-4A
Explores adult features–touch, poke, probe and/or pat; also in Strand 5-2
Displays stranger anxiety–actively resists, rejects, reaches for or moves to parent, or cries if stranger tries to hold, play with or looks at the child; N/A if appropriately not present

Shows anxiety over separation from parent–e.g., crying; searching; diminished activity level; somber mood; quick "recovery" when parents return, e.g., promptly smiles to greet, shows a toy, moves close to parent, stops crying; A if strong prolonged ambivalent responses; N/A if appropriately not present
Lets only parent meet his needs–e.g., refuses to let others feed, dress, comfort when parent present; N/A if appropriately not present

Explores environment enthusiastically–comfortable moving away from parent to explore, but also looks back to make sure parent is still there; A if no apparent concern where parent is, or preoccupation with objects
Likes to be in constant sight and hearing of adult–A if extreme, hyper-vigilant, or never concerned
Attempts self-direction: resists adult control–e.g., wants to feed or dress self; ignores "no"; A if always hostile or never requests parents as resource when faced with problem

Displays dependent behavior; clings/whines–periodic bouts of clinging/whining but still able to exercise independence and move away from parent to explore; A if excessive, usually angry or hostile, or apathetic
Feels strongly possessive of loved ones–e.g., may verbalize "my mommy!"; A if excessive, hostile
Separates easily in familiar surroundings–note any history of trauma or negative separation experiences

Shows independence: runs ahead of parent outdoors, refuses to have hand held–any behaviors indicative of urge to be independent and move away from his parent; A if overly aggressive, hits, never complies
Insists on doing things independently–e.g., "I do it!", "No", at mealtimes, dressing, and making a toy work
Inspects own hands–to watch the movements he can make; A if older and preoccupied; also in 1-4C and 4-3
Plays with own hands, feet, fingers, toes–more than merely visual inspection, e.g., clasps, touches
Makes approach movements to mirror–e.g., staring intently, brightening, reaching with head, body or hands
Explores adult features–pats, touches to explore; learning parent is separate; also in Strand 5-1
Looks and vocalizes to own name–may not vocalize; readily turns or looks to speaker; A if hears and knows name but never or rarely responds; also Strand 2-1A
Smiles at mirror image–or brightens; recognizes image as a baby or friend but may not know it is self
Distinguishes self as separate from parent–relies heavily on clinical judgment
Responds playfully to mirror–laughs, pats, makes faces, mouths, vocalizes as if this is a new friend; may not know it is self yet
Shows like/dislike for certain people, objects, places–note preferences and dislikes to help plan
Displays independent behavior; is difficult to discipline–the "no" stage; e.g., pushing things away; purposeful non-compliance; A if hostile and predominate
Shows toy preferences–e.g., always choosing a certain toy when several are available or holding on to certain toys; A if perseverates, 'tunes-out', preoccupied
Enjoys being center of attention–e.g., acts silly, performs a new skill; A if only uses hostile, aggressive means
Recognizes several people in addition to immediate family–looks for or points to
Identifies self in mirror–points to image, or removes sticker (wipes dirt, etc.) from face when seen in the mirror
Uses own name to refer to self–formal or "nick name" or an approximation; also in Strand 2-3
Experiences a strong sense of self-importance–the "me" stage
Recognizes self in photograph–at least two pictures are available
Uses "self-centered" pronouns–self-centered, i.e., "I," "me," "mine"; also in Strand 2-3
Values own property; uses word "mine"–saying "mine" when holding/guarding one of his things
Takes pride in clothing–e.g., smiles/points to his clothes
Becoming aware of sex differences–points to "boy," "girl" on request; not always correct
Distinguishes self as a separate person; contrasts self with others–e.g., upset about getting little cuts or bruises; more modest; upset over broken doll/injured person
Knows own sex or sex of others–responds correctly when asked "Are you a boy or girl?"
Takes pride in achievements; resists help–e.g., smiling, clapping etc. when accomplishing a task
Tells own age–may hold up correct number of fingers
Names siblings–when asked, "What is your sister's [brother's] name?"
Names a friend or a pet–when asked, "What is the name of your friend [or pet]?"
Cries when hungry or uncomfortable
Smiles reflexively–not specific to social interaction
Responds with smile when socially approached–purposefully and easily if content, whole face smiles
Laughs–in response to a pleasurable interaction to express delight
Squeals–loud sudden bursts of vowel sounds to express delight, happiness, excitement
Vocalizes attitudes–pleasure and displeasure; changes in voice quality, tone, inflections, facial expressions; not limited to crying or laughing
Responds to facial expressions–"reads" and responds to emotional intent of speaker, e.g., smiles to adult smile, upset to angry expression
May show fear and insecurity with previously accepted situations–common fears: animals, bath tub, hair washing, vacuum cleaners, dark places, things that break; N/A if not present
Displays frequent tantrum behaviors–A if frequent (more than 6 times per day) and prolonged (more than 10 minutes) and has difficulty recovering; N/A if not present
Hugs and kisses parents–initiates or responds
Expresses affection–range of affectionate expressions
Shows jealousy at attention given to others, especially other family member–may regress, become aggressive, whiny, dependent; N/A if not present
Shows a wide variety of emotions, e.g., fear, anger, sympathy, modesty, guilt, joy–not limited to concrete expressions such as hitting, biting, tantrums

Feels easily frustrated–by own inabilities and adult limits but should be able to handle some frustration, e.g., tries different method to make toy work, and be able to recover and move on; A if predominates throughout day
Attempts to comfort others in distress–e.g., cries or pats others when they cry

Frustration tantrums peak–usually related to situation where child feels out of control; A if frequent (more than 6 times per day) and prolonged (more than 10 minutes) and has difficulty recovering; N/A if not present
Dramatizes using a doll–e.g., expresses own feelings/emotions during doll play, e.g., soothes, hits, throws, hugs; also 1-1
Fatigues easily–may be cranky, cling after extended periods of play; N/A if not present
May develop sudden fears, especially of large animals–e.g., monsters, dark, thunderstorms, things that break, plus in #5.29 above; should not interfere with daily functioning; N/A if not present

Demonstrates extreme emotional shifts and paradoxical responses–has "up" and "down" days; may say one thing but mean another; A if predominate, unable to modulate behavior throughout day; N/A if not present
Remains calm if goals cannot be reached–usually; e.g., cannot find favorite book, accepts different one

Controls temper well, verbalizes feelings in appropriate manner–usually, e.g., "That's my toy, give it back." "That's not okay, I was playing with the car." "I don't like it when you take my book"; note if language delays impact

Tries again when a change or disappointment occurs–e.g., accepts block structure being accidentally knocked over and begins building something else; puzzle piece missing, so he moves on to another puzzle
Shows anticipatory excitement–e.g., excites when he sees bottle because he knows he will be fed
Becomes aware of strange situations–e.g., initially stares, quiets, looks around intently
Distinguishes between friendly and angry voices–e.g., cries, quiets to angry voice, smiles to friendly voice
Tests parental reactions during feeding–e.g., throwing food or utensils; spitting, playing with food; N/A if not present
Tests parental reactions at bedtime–e.g., scream, throw things; refuse to sleep; also in Strand 6-4; N/A if not present
Knows what "no no" means and reacts–briefly inhibits, cries, puckers lip, etc.; also in Strand 2-1
Acts impulsively, unable to recognize rules–A if extreme or predominates; N/A if not observed
Hands toy back to adult–learns adults can be used as resources; also in Strand 1-4C
Needs and expects rituals and routines–e.g., only eats with certain bib; only sleeps after story; mark A if excessive need for sameness, interferes with daily functioning
Begins to show a sense of humor–laughs at incongruities; e.g., hat on a dog; talking cat on television
Displays distractible behavior–but should be able to attend for 5 minutes several times a day; A if extreme, 'flits', focus on 'background noise'; N/A if not observed
Tends to be quite messy–not concerned with neatness, e.g., during feeding, play; A if excessive concern for order, clean; N/A if not observed
Desires control of others–orders, fights, resists–to "test" limits and make own "rules"; A if aggressive, hostile behavior predominates
Remembers where objects belong–puts some toys/objects away; adult assistance
Demonstrates awareness of class routines–with environmental or verbal cues; e.g., song-snack time; outdoor play after circle time
Holds parent's hand outdoors–understands rule for safety
Says "no," but submits anyway–complies about 50 percent of time even if he says no
Dawdles and procrastinates–purposefully takes extra time to complete a task or respond when called; not hostile or overtly defiant; N/A if not observed
Begins to obey and respect simple rules–understands consequences; tries to please; A if predominant, longterm defiant behavior
Resists change; is extremely ritualistic–mark A if extreme; N/A if not observed
Experiences difficulty with transitions–e.g., between sleeping and awakening; indoor and outdoor play; should not be extreme; A if extreme; N/A if not present
