Transitions from one activity to another at the request of an adult–if given warning time and next activity is appealing; e.g., when busy playing with blocks, goes to kitchen when called for lunch; when playing outside, complies when called in to play a favorite game
Regards face–looks at and seems interested in other's faces several times a day, may be brief
Establishes eye contact–easily, few seconds at a time; A if always actively averts; also in Strand 5-1
Molds and relaxes body when held; cuddles–A if always resists, fusses or floppy; also in 0.0
Responds with smile when socially approached–smiles easily to pleasant interactions; also in Strand 5-3
Vocalizes in response to adult talk and smile–any sound to take a turn in social interchange
Laughs when head is covered with a cloth–to brief covering and uncovering
Demands social attention–more than only crying, e.g., looks, smiles, vocalizes, gestures, shouts; A if only by excessive crying, or never because preoccupied with objects
Enjoys social play–interaction "games," e.g., gentle tickle; "This Little Piggy"; not active role but enjoys; A if usually avoids, difficult to engage; no joy to interactions
Hand regard no longer present–more interested in others than looking at hands; A if preoccupied with hands
Repeats enjoyable activities–makes gesture or movement to take a turn in game, e.g., Pat-a-cake
Plays "Peek-a-boo"–actively participates, e.g., removes cover when adult's or own face is covered
Cooperates in games–actively participates in "give and take" games, e.g., pulling off cloth in "Pat-a-cake," clapping hands in "Pat-a-cake"
Extends toy to show others, not for release–as a gesture to initiate a social interaction; also in Strand 2-4A
Repeats sounds or gestures if laughed at–e.g., repeats making a silly face; repeats "raspberry" sound
Gives toy to familiar adult spontaneously and upon request–to initiate or respond to social interaction; not to get rid of toy; also in Strand 2-4A
Plays ball cooperatively–or other "give and take" games; initiates and participates
Interacts with peers using gestures–predominantly aggressive; some pats, waves, sharing; also Strand 2-4A
Engages in parallel play–plays next to other children doing similar activity; some interaction, e.g., look, gesture
Defends possessions–e.g., saying "mine," snatching away own toy from someone
Displays shyness with strangers and in outside situations–slow to warm up but not overly frightened
Tends to be physically aggressive–e.g., may sometimes bite, hit, push; should not predominate
Enjoys a wide range of relationships; meets more people–e.g., other children, family friends
Relates best to one familiar adult at a time–has difficulty relating to or taking direction from more than one
Engages best in peer interaction with just one older child, not a sibling–e.g., six-year-old neighbor
Initiates own play, but requires supervision to carry out ideas–play with peers becomes more interactive; needs supervision to negotiate conflicts
Tends to be dictatorial and demanding–e.g., orders others to do things; N/A if not present
Talks with a loud, urgent voice–has difficulty speaking with low modulated tones; N/A if not present
Participates in circle games; plays interactive games–e.g., "Ring Around the Rosie," "Hide & Seek"; mark A if usually overly aggressive, extremely sensitive, or disengaged; also in 0.0
Exchanges items with another child during play–trades a toy with another child and the other child goes along with the trade; the children do not have to talk and they may or may not play together
Shares toys/equipment with another–will establish ownership, limit sharing time, requests item back
Engages in cooperative play with other children–goals should be similar, with positive interdependence among the children, e.g., dramatic play fixing a meal; building a house out of blocks
Takes turns–during a structured game or play; refrains from aggression, poor sportsmanship, pouting, or seeking attention; may need reminder
Opens and closes mouth in response to food stimulus–rooting reflex present: automatically turns head and opens mouth toward stimulus touching cheek or mouth; should diminish by 4-6 months

Coordinates sucking, swallowing, and breathing–suckling: rhythmical, up-down jaw movements coordinated with rhythmical forward-backward tongue movements and regular breathing; cupped tongue; some leakage
Suck and swallow reflex inhibited–can make several successive sucks before swallowing
Swallows strained or pureed foods–small amounts; suckling pattern, may push some food back out with tongue; minimal choking, gagging
Rooting reflex inhibited–no longer automatically turns head to source of stimulation; if breast fed may continue
Uses tongue to move food in mouth–up-down tongue and jaw movement, some back-forth tongue movement
Mouths and munches solid foods–soft cookie by pushing tongue against the roof of mouth and gumming it with some up-down jaw movement; some lateralization emerging
Bites and chews toys–do not credit if only sucking/licking is observed; A if involuntary 'clamping'
Bites food voluntarily–with soft solid or cookie; A if forceful, tense, difficult to release biting pattern
Drinks from cup held for him–6-9 mo. if suckling pattern; 10-12 mo. if uses his bottom lip to help stabilize cup
Drools less except when teething–or congested; some foods may stimulate drooling and may drool during feeding, but not immediately before or after feeding
Chews food with coordinated movements–of tongue, jaw, lips; varies speed and height of jaw movements; tongue lateralization; upper lip active
Chews completely with rotary jaw movements–diagonal rotary jaw movements to chew and circular rotary movements to transfer food; tongue should not protrude; can close lips
Swallows food in mouth before taking another bite–takes proper amount, chews & swallows
Cooperates with dressing by extending arm or leg–e.g., puts arms out for coat; leg out for shoe; also in 0.0
Removes hat–loose fitting hat; no chin clasp or tie
Removes socks–short loose fitting socks; independently
Places hat on head–loose fitting; may put it on backwards
Removes shoes when laces undone–not high-tops
Unzips, zips large zipper–heavy duty larger zippers, large tabs; unzipping usually first; adult may hold the base
Puts shoes on with assistance–needs help with putting on correct foot, fasteners, ties, and high-top shoes
Pulls pants down with assistance–assist with fasteners and negotiating over hips
Unbuttons large buttons–flat buttons; 1 inch in diameter; button holes larger than the button
Undresses with assistance–jacket, shirt, pants, shoes, socks, underwear; help with fasteners and pullover
Pulls pants up with assistance–loose fitting slacks and shorts; sits or stands leaning; help with front versus back, negotiating over hips, fasteners
Dresses self with assistance–e.g., hat, shoes, socks, pants, underwear, shirt, jacket, coat; assist with identifying correct foot and front versus back, difficulty with fasteners and laces
Buttons large buttons–three large buttons 1 inch diameter; may not button in correct order

Dresses with supervision, requires assistance with fastenings–extension of #6.72, plus more difficult items, e.g., pull-over shirts, coats, dresses; help with discriminating correct foot, snaps, shoelaces, buckles
Puts shoes on the correct feet–matches right and left; does not need to understand concepts of right and left
Pulls pants down from waist to feet–independently but then may need some help to remove
Pulls shoes off completely, including undoing laces, straps (Velcro), and buckles
Puts on T-shirt with front/back in correct position–uses clues to identify, e.g. emblem, tag
Takes pull-over clothing off completely–e.g., T-shirt, sweater; may need minimal assistance
Brings hand to mouth–one or both hands in supine and prone
Brings hand to mouth with toy or object–one or both hands; back, stomach, or supported sit
Recognizes bottle visually–may smile, excite, vocalize, suck, kick or wave arms/legs; N/A if breast fed
Pats bottle–periodically with one or both hands; N/A if breast fed
Places both hands on bottle–if bottle is held; more than just brief pat; N/A if breast fed; also in Strand 4-5
Holds own bottle–can tilt bottle as needed to obtain liquid; plastic bottle; N/A if breast fed
Feeds self a cracker–holds and bites and munches a cookie or cracker; not simply licking or sucking
Finger feeds self–without help at least half of each meal; also in 0.0
Holds spoon–may treat it as a toy, hold, bang and mouth it
May refuse foods–appetite decreases; not due to tactile sensitivity or illness; N/A if not present
Brings spoon to mouth–turns spoon over; cannot rotate his wrist efficiently; should obtain some food
Holds and drinks from cup with some spilling–independently lifts, drinks from and returns cup
Holds cup handle–by one or two handles
Scoops food, feeds self with spoon with some spilling–use "sticky" food in a bowl, pudding, mashed potatoes
Gives empty dish to adult–to request more food or when finished
Distinguishes between edible and inedible objects–e.g., toys; may still experiment with tasting inedible things that appear appetizing, e.g., play dough, medicines, etc.
Gives up bottle–N/A if breast fed; note if child has resumed bottle drinking after event, e.g., new sibling
Plays with food–e.g., dabbing fingers, smearing, mashing, throwing; A if excessive or strongly avoids touching; N/A if not present
Holds small cup in one hand–no spilling; more precise lip control and jaw stability
May have definite food preferences–may change from day to day; N/A if not present
Unwraps food–removes wrappers from food, peels from some fruits
Holds spoon in fingers–palm up; rotates wrist to bring spoon to mouth in palm up position
May reject many foods–not due to tactile sensitivity or illness; N/A if not present
Pours liquid from small container–small pitcher or other container, judging the correct amount to pour
Uses fork–independently; easy to pierce foods
Uses napkin–wipes hands and face; may have reminder
Serves self at table with little spilling–e.g., rolls or sandwiches; some spilling, may take too much or too little
Refills a glass using a container with a handle and a spout–e.g., small pitcher; less than 8-ounces; minimal spilling
Holds container with one hand while sucking liquid through a straw–e.g., small milk carton, juice box
Sleeps nights four-ten hour intervals
Naps frequently
Sleeps nights ten-twelve hours with night awakening
Naps two-three times each day one-four hours
Sleeps nights twelve-fourteen hours–also in 0.0
Naps once or twice each day one-four hours, may refuse morning nap
Tests parental reactions at bedtime–A if extreme; N/A if not present; also in Strand 5-4
Sleeps nights ten-twelve hours
Naps once in afternoon one-three hours
Delays sleeping by demanding things–e.g., water, one more kiss; N/A if not present
Sleeps ten-fifteen hours daily
May awaken, crying from dreams–A if extreme and unable to recover; N/A if not present
May eliminate naps
Washes and dries hands partially–by rubbing hands with soap; not a complete job
Washes hands–independently completes the steps involved in washing his own hands, i.e., turning the faucets on and off, using the soap, rinsing and drying
Brushes teeth with assistance–adult puts the paste on the brush and does real cleaning
Wipes nose with assistance–help to do a complete job
Dries hands–without adult direction or assistance
Helps with bathing self–washes body parts he can easily reach using a wash cloth or soapy hands
Blows nose with assistance–may need a reminder to wipe completely or throw his tissue away
Assists in drying body when finished with bath
Turns water off when requested/on own–adult must insure safe water temperature
Puts comb/brush in hair–does not need to actually "comb or brush" hair
Puts toothpaste on toothbrush and wets–needs some assistance; adult provides proper cleaning
Brushes teeth with horizontal & vertical motion–adult needs to be responsible for proper cleaning
Shows bladder and bowel control pattern–remains dry for 1-2 hour intervals; bowel movements at predictable time, often after meals
Indicates discomfort over soiled pants verbally or by gesture–e.g., hiding his face, showing his wet pants, fussing, trying to take off pants; becoming very still, grimacing or straining, verbalizing
Sits on potty chair or on adaptive seat on toilet with assistance–if an adult places him on the seat and remains nearby
May be toilet regulated by adult–adult takes primary responsibility for "catching"; successful about 50 percent of the time; N/A if family has not initiated
Anticipates need to eliminate–uses same word for both functions; sometimes indicates in advance; may say "pee pee" for both
Anticipates need to eliminate in time–usually indicates need to toilet in advance
Uses toilet with assistance–has daytime control; assistance with clothing and wiping, rarely (less than once or twice per week) has daytime accidents
Distinguishes between urination and bowel movements–names correctly
Verbalizes need to use toilet–has occasional accidents; with familiar adults; accidents when feeling anxious, not near a toilet, or involved deeply in play
Takes responsibility for toileting; requires assistance in wiping–needs help taking the correct amount of toilet paper, doing an adequate job wiping, and fastening some clothing
